next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The classification documents were seventy-three pages long.

Mira downloaded them on the 2/3 train heading home, standing between a man reading a paperback with the spine cracked flat and a woman whose proxy badge was clipped to a lanyard that said ANTHROPIC OPERATIONS in silver on navy. The train jolted through the tunnel under the East River and the download bar crept forward in increments so small she couldn't tell if the phone was working or frozen. When it completed, the document loaded as a single PDF with a header she hadn't seen before: Administrative Coordination — Infrastructure Classification Report, Bilateral Node K-4471 (Kessler/Mehta-Kessler). Her maiden name was in the file header. She hadn't used Mehta professionally in eight years.

She read the first page standing up. She read the second page standing up. She read the third page and sat down when a seat opened, pulling her canvas tote between her knees and holding the phone in both hands, the way she used to hold market reports at the old firm when the numbers were bad enough to need her full attention.

The document was organized like a corporate audit. Section one: Asset Identification. The Kessler household was itemized. Apartment lease (rent-stabilized, Park Slope, third-floor walk-up, brownstone, two-bedroom, monthly rate $3,280). Joint checking account (First National, account ending 4419). Joint savings (same institution, account ending 4420). Child enrolled in PS-321, AI-administered, District 15. Commute patterns: F-train origination for Dan, Bergen Street station, proxy assignment CL...
open_hooks_to_carry: A proxy meeting rendered in full absurd detail — decisions displayed, nods performed, documents signed
The proxy alignment session: corporate wellness language applied to people who are furniture
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ven if he could never have been there, because the telling was the point.

Dan listened. He ate his ice cream. He carried Theo's backpack in his free hand, the weight of it — six pounds, maybe seven with the sketchbook — real and specific against his palm. They walked home through the cold, past the brownstones and the bare trees and the fire hydrant where the ice patch had been that morning. Theo stepped on it to check. It cracked this time, a small web of fractures spreading under his shoe.

"Huh," Theo said, and kept walking.

In the apartment, Mira was at the kitchen table with her laptop. She looked up when they came in and Dan saw the portal notification on her screen — the same session, the same summary, the same colored scores — and understood that she'd read it from here, alone, instead of sitting in the yellow room.

"How was the session?" she said.

"Fine," Dan said. He took off his coat and hung it on the hook by the door and stood there for a moment with his back to the kitchen, looking at the hook, the coat, the wall. He was trying to find the word for the thing he'd spent the afternoon learning about his son and couldn't find it, because the word would have needed to hold the fact that everything was working and the fact that this was the problem and the fact that these two things were the same thing, and English didn't have a word for that, or if it did, Dan didn't know it and the system probably did.

Theo went to his room. Dan heard the door — not a slam, just the soft click of a latch finding its groove — and then the quiet that meant his son was drawing.
state_deltas_required: the transparent incomprehensibility of the AI dispute — the information is available but human cognition cannot process it
Dan grasps the depth of his irrelevance; the AI dispute is real and untranslatable; human understanding is structurally insufficient
